stem:
A 14-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department on day 6 of a fever that has reached 39.4 degrees twice daily and has not responded to regular paracetamol. The parents report a blotchy red rash that came up on his trunk on day 3 and his hands now look puffy. He has been miserable, off feeds, and clingy.

On examination he is well perfused but irritable. There is bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection sparing the limbus, a strawberry-coloured tongue with cracked lips, a polymorphous trunk rash, and a single 1.8 cm right cervical lymph node. The throat is mildly erythematous without exudate or palatal petechiae. Capillary return is brisk. There is no recent overseas travel.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 158, blood pressure 92/54, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 39.2 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 102 g/L (105-135), platelets 612 x10^9/L (150-450), white cells 18.4 x10^9/L (5-15), CRP 142 mg/L (<5), ESR 78 mm/hr, albumin 28 g/L (35-50), ALT 92 U/L (<40), sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), urinalysis sterile pyuria, throat swab pending, rapid strep antigen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours with oral aspirin 30 mg/kg per day in four divided doses
options.rationale: Five clinical criteria are met (fever five or more days, bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, oropharyngeal changes, polymorphous rash, hand swelling, cervical node greater than 1.5 cm) supported by classical adjunctive laboratory findings (CRP > 30, platelets > 450, albumin < 30, sterile pyuria, transaminitis, normocytic anaemia). IVIG within 10 days of fever onset reduces coronary aneurysm risk from around 25 percent to under 5 percent. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily for ten days under a working diagnosis of streptococcal scarlet fever
options.rationale: Bacterial scarlet fever is excluded by the absent exudative pharyngitis, negative rapid strep antigen, and the constellation of mucocutaneous features. Oral cefalexin delays IVIG and exposes the child to ongoing coronary artery inflammation. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Arrange outpatient transthoracic echocardiography in two weeks and continue regular paracetamol with parental safety netting
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral allows coronary artery inflammation to evolve unchecked. Echocardiography is part of the diagnostic workup but does not replace prompt IVIG when clinical criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for three days as monotherapy without concurrent immunoglobulin
options.rationale: High dose corticosteroids are reserved for IVIG resistance or for primary therapy intensification in high risk cases (Kobayashi-style scoring is not validated outside Japanese populations). Steroid monotherapy without IVIG is not standard primary therapy in Australia. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Send blood cultures and start intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every six hours for presumed staphylococcal toxin illness
options.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin or toxic shock would show bullae or hypotension and the rash pattern here is the polymorphous exanthem of Kawasaki. Flucloxacillin treats neither Kawasaki nor the small chance of group A streptococcal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis of young children whose most feared complication is coronary artery aneurysm. The classical criteria are fever five or more days plus four of: bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, polymorphous rash, oropharyngeal changes, extremity changes, and cervical node greater than 1.5 cm. Incomplete Kawasaki (two or three features plus supportive labs) is treated identically. IVIG 2 g/kg plus high dose aspirin within ten days of fever onset is the Australian standard.
key_points: Fever five or more days plus four classical features (or two or three plus supportive labs) triggers IVIG
Aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg per day in four divided doses until fever resolves, then 3 to 5 mg/kg per day for six to eight weeks
Coronary aneurysm risk falls from around 25 percent to under 5 percent with timely IVIG
Baseline and six to eight week echocardiography is mandatory
Sterile pyuria, transaminitis, low albumin, and high platelets support the diagnosis
explanation.pearls: If IVIG is given within 10 days of fever onset the absolute risk of coronary artery aneurysm at six to eight week echo drops from roughly 25 percent (untreated) to under 5 percent.

stem:
A 3-year-old previously well girl is brought to a regional emergency department with three days of fever to 39.6 degrees, a wet cough, and the parents say she is breathing fast and has stopped drinking from her cup. She has had no vomiting and her last wet nappy was four hours ago.

On examination she is alert but tachypnoeic with mild subcostal recession. Auscultation reveals reduced breath sounds and bronchial breathing at the right base with crackles audible into the mid zone. The left chest is clear. Capillary return is two seconds and mucous membranes are moist. There is no stridor or wheeze, and no rash. She is immunised on schedule including 13-valent pneumococcal and Haemophilus vaccines.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 142, blood pressure 96/58, respiratory rate 48, SpO2 92 percent on room air, temperature 39.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: chest radiograph confirms right lower lobe consolidation without effusion, point of care blood gas within normal limits, capillary glucose 4.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit, commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg every six hours, and supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 92 percent
options.rationale: Australian first line inpatient therapy for typical bacterial CAP in a vaccinated child is intravenous benzylpenicillin; switch to oral amoxicillin once afebrile for 24 to 48 hours. The SpO2 of 92 percent with feeding compromise and tachypnoea satisfies admission criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Community Acquired Pneumonia)

options.text: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for five days with general practice review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Hypoxia (SpO2 less than 93 percent), tachypnoea above 50 in this age band, and reduced oral intake mandate inpatient management under RCH criteria. Oral therapy is appropriate only when these admission triggers are absent. (Source: RCH CPG: Community Acquired Pneumonia)

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily as monotherapy for empirical broad spectrum cover
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved for severe disease, suspected resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae, complications such as empyema, or failure of penicillin therapy. Routine empirical use drives resistance and is not first line in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily as monotherapy targeting atypical bacterial pathogens
options.rationale: Mycoplasma is uncommon under five years and lobar consolidation favours typical bacterial pathogens. Macrolide monotherapy misses Streptococcus pneumoniae and is not first line in this age band. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit on intravenous fluids alone and withhold antibiotics pending respiratory viral PCR and procalcitonin results
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics in a hypoxic febrile child with lobar consolidation risks progression and empyema. Viral PCR and procalcitonin are adjuncts and do not justify treatment delay. (Source: RCH CPG: Community Acquired Pneumonia)

explanation.summary: Paediatric community acquired pneumonia is most often pneumococcal in vaccinated under-five Australians. Admission triggers under RCH guidance include SpO2 less than 92 percent, significant respiratory distress, dehydration or inability to feed, and toxic appearance. Inpatient first line therapy is intravenous benzylpenicillin, switching to oral amoxicillin once afebrile for 24 to 48 hours. Reserve ceftriaxone for complicated disease or treatment failure.
key_points: Admission criteria: SpO2 < 92 percent, severe distress, dehydration, toxic appearance, age under 3 months, or social concerns
Inpatient first line: IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg every six hours
Step down to oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily once afebrile for 24 to 48 hours
Total course 5 to 7 days for uncomplicated lobar pneumonia
Add macrolide if atypical features (school age, dry cough, headache, interstitial pattern)
explanation.pearls: Reduced breath sounds with stony dullness despite IV benzylpenicillin for 48 hours prompts urgent chest ultrasound to detect early parapneumonic effusion before clinical decompensation.

stem:
A 9-month-old infant is brought to a tertiary emergency department with a 14 hour history of episodic distress. The parents describe him going pale and pulling his legs up, screaming for 90 seconds, then becoming floppy and quiet between episodes. He has now had two soft jelly-like dark red stools in his nappy and refused his last two feeds.

On examination he is pale but rouseable. The abdomen is soft with a faintly palpable long thin lump in the right upper quadrant. Bowel sounds are present. There are no peritonitic signs and no abdominal distension. Capillary return is two seconds. He has had no recent gastroenteritis or upper respiratory infection. There is no Henoch-Schonlein purpura rash.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 144, blood pressure 88/52, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 37.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.36 base excess negative 2, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, point of care abdominal ultrasound shows a target sign in the right upper quadrant with no free fluid and preserved bowel wall echogenicity

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge after a normal abdominal radiograph with paracetamol and family safety netting about red flag symptoms
options.rationale: Abdominal radiography is insensitive for intussusception and discharge is unsafe with the diagnosis already confirmed on ultrasound. Untreated ileocolic intussusception progresses to bowel ischaemia within 24 to 48 hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Arrange immediate laparotomy with paediatric surgery without prior attempt at radiological reduction by air enema
options.rationale: Primary laparotomy is reserved for peritonism, perforation on imaging, shock that does not respond to resuscitation, or failed radiological reduction. None of these are present here. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Resuscitate with 20 mL/kg saline, place a nasogastric tube on drainage, and arrange image guided air enema reduction
options.rationale: Air enema reduction under fluoroscopic guidance is the first line treatment for ileocolic intussusception in a haemodynamically stable child without peritonism or perforation. Success rates exceed 80 percent and surgery is reserved for failure or complications. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Admit for 24 hours nil by mouth with maintenance intravenous fluids and reassessment by surgical team in the morning
options.rationale: Observation alone allows ongoing bowel ischaemia. Definitive reduction must occur as soon as resuscitation allows. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Commence intravenous metronidazole and gentamicin and proceed to immediate diagnostic laparoscopy regardless of imaging
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics and primary laparoscopy bypass radiological reduction which is non-invasive and highly effective. Antibiotics are added only when perforation or established sepsis is suspected. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Intussusception is the commonest abdominal emergency between three months and three years. The classic triad of colicky pain, palpable mass, and redcurrant stools occurs in fewer than 20 percent, so a high index of suspicion is needed. Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice (target or doughnut sign). Air enema reduction succeeds in 80 to 90 percent of stable children, with surgery reserved for failure, perforation, peritonism, or pathological lead points.
key_points: Peak incidence 3 months to 3 years; ileocolic most common
Ultrasound target sign is the diagnostic test
Air enema first line if stable, no peritonism, no perforation
Nasogastric decompression and 20 mL/kg fluid bolus before reduction
Recurrence after enema reduction is around 10 percent and may need repeat enema or surgery
explanation.pearls: After successful air enema reduction at the RCH, recurrence within 72 hours is around 10 percent, so admit for at least 24 hours of observation rather than same-day discharge.

stem:
A 7-week-old infant is brought to a metropolitan emergency department because the mother noticed bruising on his right cheek and a swollen left thigh. The family attended a community drop-in immunisation clinic earlier the same day and the nurse there raised concerns. There is no reported trauma. The mother is 19, the partner she lives with is not the biological father, and the social history records financial stress and recent eviction.

On examination the infant is alert and feeding well. There is a 2 cm purple bruise on the right zygoma and a 4 cm fusiform swelling over the mid left thigh with reluctance to weight-bear when held vertically. Fundi are clear. There is no rash, no birthmarks, and the fontanelle is soft. The remainder of the systemic examination is unremarkable.

data_table.Investigations: skeletal survey not yet performed, full blood count and coagulation studies within normal limits, capillary glucose 4.6 mmol/L, urinalysis sterile, paediatric early warning score zero

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with general practitioner review in 48 hours and a safety net to return if any new bruising appears
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury and discharge is unsafe. Cumulative risk of further inflicted injury or death within months of a missed sentinel bruise is well described. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Reassure that bruising is common at this age, request iron studies, and arrange community paediatrician review
options.rationale: Bruising in a non-cruising infant is not normal and iron studies have no diagnostic role. Outpatient deferral allows continued exposure to a potentially unsafe environment. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Request coagulation screen and platelet function tests before deciding whether to notify the child protection authority
options.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of the workup but do not delay mandatory reporting. Statutory notification is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not by exclusion of bleeding disorders. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Admit, notify statutory child protection, request skeletal survey, retinal examination, head imaging, and social work
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant ('those who don't cruise rarely bruise') mandates reporting to the state child protection service and a complete non-accidental injury workup including skeletal survey, retinal examination, head imaging, and consideration of a coagulation screen. Australian medical practitioners are mandated reporters in every state and territory. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Discuss the bruising openly with the mother and notify child protection only if she fails to give a plausible account
options.rationale: Statutory notification is mandatory once reasonable suspicion arises and does not require parental admission or denial. Delaying notification undermines the protective intent of the legislation. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: Australian doctors are mandated reporters of suspected child abuse under state and territory legislation. Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury and demands a structured non-accidental injury workup: skeletal survey (repeat in two weeks), retinal examination by ophthalmology, head imaging, coagulation screen, and social work involvement. Reasonable suspicion, not proof, is the threshold for notification.
key_points: Pre-mobile infants ('those who don't cruise rarely bruise') with bruising warrant a full NAI workup
Notify state child protection on reasonable suspicion - not after exclusion of bleeding diatheses
Skeletal survey is standard; repeat in two weeks improves sensitivity for healing fractures
Document carefully, photograph injuries with consent, escalate to senior paediatrics and social work
Coagulation studies and platelet function tests run in parallel, not before reporting
explanation.pearls: In Australian state law (including Children and Young People Act variants), the threshold for mandatory notification is reasonable suspicion of significant harm - higher than 'possible' but lower than 'probable on the balance of probabilities'.

sources.label: RACGP: Abuse and violence working with our patients in general practice

stem:
A previously well 22-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his parents found him pale and unresponsive in his cot at 6 am. He vomited twice the previous evening and went to bed without his usual milk. Paramedics measured a capillary glucose of 1.6 mmol/L and gave 2 mL/kg of 10 percent dextrose with rapid recovery of consciousness. He has had no previous similar episodes and is on the 25th centile for weight.

On examination he is now alert and playful. There is no hepatomegaly, no Cushingoid features, no hyperpigmentation, and no midline defects. Tone, reflexes, and cranial nerves are normal. The parents say he refused dinner because of the gastroenteritis but the older sister recovered without low sugars. Vital signs are within normal limits.

data_table.Investigations: critical sample drawn at glucose 1.6 mmol/L: ketones (3-hydroxybutyrate) 4.2 mmol/L, free fatty acids 2.6 mmol/L, insulin <2 mU/L, C-peptide <0.1 nmol/L, cortisol 612 nmol/L, growth hormone 18 mcg/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, ammonia 38 micromol/L (10-50), urine organic acids and acylcarnitine profile pending

lead_in: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Medium chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency presenting at this age
options.rationale: MCAD presents with hypoketotic hypoglycaemia because fatty acid oxidation is blocked. The high 3-hydroxybutyrate effectively excludes a fatty acid oxidation defect at presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Congenital hyperinsulinism due to a sulfonylurea receptor (KATP channel) mutation
options.rationale: Hyperinsulinism produces hypoketotic and non-lipolytic hypoglycaemia with detectable insulin and C-peptide at the time of low glucose. The high ketones, free fatty acids, and suppressed insulin here exclude this. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Idiopathic ketotic hypoglycaemia of childhood with appropriate counter-regulation
options.rationale: Fasting hypoglycaemia with appropriate ketosis and lipolysis, suppressed insulin and C-peptide, and a robust cortisol and growth hormone response is the textbook profile of idiopathic ketotic hypoglycaemia, peak age 18 months to 5 years. The provocation (vomiting and reduced overnight intake in a thin toddler) fits. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Glycogen storage disease type 1 (von Gierke) with subclinical hepatomegaly
options.rationale: GSD1 features hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis with hypoglycaemia, and elevated triglycerides and uric acid. Absent hepatomegaly and a normal lactate at the time of hypoglycaemia argue against this diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Adrenal insufficiency with subclinical cortisol deficiency exacerbated by vomiting
options.rationale: Adrenal insufficiency produces hypoglycaemia with an inappropriately low cortisol (under 500 nmol/L) at the time of the event. The cortisol of 612 nmol/L documents a normal stress response. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

explanation.summary: The 'critical sample' drawn at the moment of hypoglycaemia is the most informative investigation in a paediatric hypoglycaemia workup because it preserves the metabolic snapshot. Appropriate ketosis with suppressed insulin and intact counter-regulation defines idiopathic ketotic hypoglycaemia, a diagnosis of exclusion peaking between 18 months and 5 years and self-limiting by school age. The cornerstone of management is avoidance of prolonged fasting and early carbohydrate during intercurrent illness.
key_points: Critical sample: glucose, ketones, free fatty acids, insulin, C-peptide, cortisol, GH, lactate, ammonia, urine organic acids, acylcarnitines
Hyperinsulinism: hypoketotic, hypo-lipolytic, detectable insulin/C-peptide
Fatty acid oxidation defect: hypoketotic with high free fatty acids
GSD1: hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis, hyperlipidaemia
Adrenal insufficiency: cortisol < 500 nmol/L at the time of hypoglycaemia
explanation.pearls: Idiopathic ketotic hypoglycaemia is a diagnosis of exclusion - if hypoglycaemia first occurs after age 5, MCAD or another fatty acid oxidation disorder must be actively excluded by acylcarnitine profile and urine organic acids before applying the label.

sources.label: APEG: Paediatric Endocrine Society of Australia (APEG) Hypoglycaemia

stem:
A 9-year-old girl presents to a tertiary paediatric emergency department with two weeks of fatigue, polyuria, and nocturnal wetting, and one day of vomiting. The mother says she has lost weight despite eating more, and today she looked drowsy. There is no recent overseas travel and no family history of diabetes.

On examination she is drowsy but rouseable, breathing deeply, with dry mucous membranes and capillary return three seconds. There is no abdominal tenderness, no rash, and no signs of cerebral oedema. Pulse rate 132, blood pressure 96/58, respiratory rate 36, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees. Her actual body weight on arrival is 28 kg. A point of care capillary blood gas confirms a severe metabolic acidosis with hyperglycaemia and high ketones.

data_table.Investigations: venous pH 7.06, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, base excess negative 22, glucose 28 mmol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 5.8 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145) corrected sodium 138, potassium 4.3 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 9.2 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, urinalysis 4+ glucose 4+ ketones

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid plan in the first hour?

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg saline bolus over 15 minutes then commence intravenous insulin infusion immediately at 0.1 units/kg/hour
options.rationale: Rapid 20 mL/kg boluses are reserved for shock; this child has compensated tachycardia and capillary refill three seconds (consistent with around 7 to 10 percent dehydration but not shocked). Bolusing 20 mL/kg over 15 minutes risks cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Replace the calculated fluid deficit over six hours alongside maintenance fluids to correct dehydration rapidly
options.rationale: The total deficit plus maintenance is replaced evenly over 48 hours in paediatric DKA. Six hour replacement accelerates osmotic shifts and increases the risk of cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately without preceding fluid bolus resuscitation
options.rationale: Insulin before adequate fluid resuscitation causes a rapid intracellular potassium shift, intravascular volume depletion, and increases cerebral oedema risk. Australian protocols require fluid first and insulin no earlier than one hour after fluid commencement. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 60 minutes to correct severe acidaemia before any other intervention
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is contraindicated in paediatric DKA except in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or cardiac arrest unresponsive to standard resuscitation. It worsens cerebral oedema and paradoxical CSF acidosis. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guidelines)

options.text: Give a 10 mL/kg saline bolus over 30 to 60 minutes, withhold insulin for the first hour, and reassess perfusion at one hour
options.rationale: APEG/ISPAD-aligned RCH protocols give a single 10 mL/kg saline bolus over 30 to 60 minutes for moderate to severe DKA without shock, withhold insulin for the first hour to allow rehydration, then commence insulin at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour. This minimises rapid osmotic shifts and cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA is defined by pH less than 7.3 or bicarbonate less than 15, hyperglycaemia, and ketonaemia. The greatest threat is cerebral oedema, which carries roughly 25 percent mortality and 35 percent neurological morbidity. Modern Australian protocols (APEG, RCH) align with ISPAD: cautious rehydration with one 10 mL/kg saline bolus (only repeat for shock), insulin no earlier than one hour after fluid commencement at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour, deficit replacement over 48 hours, hourly neuro observations, and bicarbonate only for arrest or severe hyperkalaemia.
key_points: Diagnose DKA: pH < 7.3 or bicarbonate < 15, glucose > 11, ketones > 3 mmol/L
Single 10 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline bolus for moderate-severe DKA without shock
Insulin 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour from one hour after fluid started
Replace deficit plus maintenance evenly over 48 hours
Cerebral oedema warning signs: headache, falling Glasgow Coma Score, hypertension, bradycardia - give 2.7 percent saline or mannitol immediately
explanation.pearls: Cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA peaks 4 to 12 hours after starting fluids - hourly neurological observations and a low threshold for hypertonic saline 2.7 percent at 2.5 to 5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg are mandatory.
